skill.name: pdb-database
skill.description: Access the RCSB Protein Data Bank (PDB) to search, download, and programmatically retrieve 3D macromolecular structures and metadata; use when you need structure discovery (text/sequence/3D similarity) or automated structural data ingestion for structural biology and drug discovery workflows.
skill.summary: Programmatic access to the RCSB Protein Data Bank for searching, downloading, and analyzing 3D protein structures and associated metadata.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported 167 potential findings, but nearly all are false positives from documentation code examples and scientific data fields misidentified by the scanner. One confirmed low-risk finding: subprocess.run with hardcoded arguments appears in debugging documentation. All network calls target legitimate RCSB PDB endpoints, and file write operations are for standard PDB coordinate downloads. No evidence of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or command injection was found.

low_findings.title: External command execution via subprocess in documentation
low_findings.description: subprocess.run is called with curl in a debugging example. Arguments are hardcoded strings (PDB ID 4HHB and a fixed URL), so there is no command injection risk. This is a documentation example in the API reference guide, not active code.

content.user_title: Access and Retrieve 3D Protein Structures from RCSB PDB
content.value_statement: Structural biology researchers often waste hours navigating the RCSB PDB website for protein structure data. This skill provides programmatic access to search, download, and analyze 3D macromolecular structures directly from your AI chat.
actual_capabilities: Search the RCSB PDB by text keywords, organism name, experimental method, and resolution range
Find related protein structures using sequence similarity with configurable E-value and identity thresholds
Download 3D coordinate files in PDB and mmCIF formats for downstream analysis
Retrieve detailed metadata including resolution, method, deposit date, and molecular weight via the RCSB Data API
Perform batch retrieval of metadata and coordinates from multiple PDB IDs in one workflow
Combine search filters using AND, OR, and NOT logic for precise structure discovery
limitations: Cannot analyze or visualize 3D structures directly; coordinates must be processed with external tools like PyMOL or ChimeraX
Limited to publicly available PDB entries only; does not access user-private structural data
Not designed for real-time molecular dynamics simulation or docking calculations
Large batch downloads may be subject to RCSB rate limiting and require delay between requests

use_cases.title: Structure-Based Drug Discovery
use_cases.description: A medicinal chemist searches for high-resolution kinase structures bound to specific ligands, downloads coordinates, and prepares them for docking studies.

use_cases.title: Homology Modeling Pipeline Setup
use_cases.description: A structural biologist finds the best template structures by sequence similarity search, retrieves their metadata and coordinates for protein model building.

use_cases.title: Educational Structural Biology
use_cases.description: A biochemistry instructor retrieves example protein structures by keyword and organism, downloads them in PDB format for classroom visualization activities.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Protein Structure Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the Protein Data Bank for hemoglobin structures from Homo sapiens with resolution better than 2.0 angstroms. Return the top 5 PDB IDs with their titles and resolutions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Introductory search by keyword and attribute filter

prompt_templates.title: Sequence-Based Homolog Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this protein sequence to find similar structures in the PDB: [paste sequence]. Set the E-value cutoff to 0.001 and identity cutoff to 0.7. Return matching PDB IDs and their titles.
prompt_templates.scenario: Finding related structures by protein sequence similarity

prompt_templates.title: Batch Metadata and Coordinate Download
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch metadata for these PDB IDs: 4HHB, 1A3N, 2DN1. For each entry, provide the title, experimental method, resolution, and deposit date. Then download the PDB coordinate files for the first two entries.
prompt_templates.scenario: Automated batch processing for multiple structures

prompt_templates.title: Complex Filtered Search with Structure Similarity
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all human protein kinase structures with resolution better than 2.5 angstroms that were released in 2024. For the top result, perform a 3D structure similarity search to identify proteins with similar folds. Return the full list of related structures.

output_examples.input: Search for high-resolution chaperonin structures from E. coli
output_examples.output:
Found 12 structures matching chaperonin from Escherichia coli with resolution below 3.0 angstroms.

Top results:
- 1AON: GroEL-GroES Complex, 2.8 A, X-RAY DIFFRACTION
- 7KSO: GroEL with AMPPNP, 2.9 A, X-RAY DIFFRACTION
- 6S82: GroEL apical domain, 2.5 A, X-RAY DIFFRACTION

Downloaded coordinates for 1AON to pdb_out/1AON.pdb and pdb_out/1AON.cif.

Entry 4HHB - Hemoglobin (Deoxyhemoglobin)
Experimental method: X-RAY DIFFRACTION
Resolution: 1.74 A
Deposit date: 1985-03-05
Source organism: Homo sapiens

The structure contains 4 chains (A, B, C, D) with 4772 atoms total.
Coordinates downloaded to 4HHB.pdb and 4HHB.cif.

output_examples.input: Find structures similar to this KRAS sequence
output_examples.output:
Sequence similarity search found 3 closely related structures (E-value less than 0.001, identity greater than 90 percent).

- 5V9U: KRAS G12C mutant in complex with ARS-1620, 1.7 A
- 6P2D: KRAS G12D mutant, 2.0 A
- 8OBQ: KRAS G12V mutant, 1.9 A

All three are oncogenic mutants of human KRAS with high-quality crystallographic data suitable for drug design studies.

best_practices: Always use the Search API first to identify relevant PDB IDs before fetching full metadata. This minimizes API calls and reduces bandwidth usage.
Implement rate limiting with a 0.5 to 1 second delay between requests when processing large batches to stay within RCSB server limits.
Use the mmCIF format instead of legacy PDB format for large or complex structures. It handles multi-chain entries and non-standard residues more completely.
anti_patterns: Do not download the same PDB file repeatedly in loops. Cache downloaded files locally and check for their existence before making new requests.
Avoid sending extremely long protein sequences over 1000 residues without first verifying the sequence is valid and trimmed to the domain of interest.
Do not set E-value cutoffs above 10 in sequence similarity searches. This returns thousands of low-confidence matches that are not biologically meaningful.

faq.question: What is the RCSB Protein Data Bank?
faq.answer: It is a global repository of 3D structural data for proteins, nucleic acids, and complex macromolecules determined by X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy, and cryo-electron microscopy.

faq.question: Do I need to install any Python packages to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. You need the rcsb-api and requests packages. Biopython is optional but recommended for parsing downloaded PDB files.

faq.question: What file formats are available for download?
faq.answer: PDB format (legacy text-based) and mmCIF format (modern standard, also called PDBx). mmCIF is recommended for large or complex structures.

faq.question: Can I search by protein sequence instead of keywords?
faq.answer: Yes. Use a SequenceQuery to find structures by amino acid or nucleotide sequence similarity, with configurable E-value and identity cutoffs.

faq.question: Is there a limit on how many searches I can make?
faq.answer: RCSB PDB APIs implement rate limiting to ensure fair use. Start with a few requests per second and use exponential backoff if you receive 429 errors.

faq.question: Can I search for structures by 3D shape similarity?
faq.answer: Yes. Use StructSimilarityQuery with an existing PDB entry, chain, or assembly as the reference to find geometrically similar structures via BioZernike descriptors.
